revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Altägyptische Kryptographie]] The term '''Ancient Egyptian Cryptography''' refers to {{ill|Egyptology|en|Egyptology|qid=Q145903|lt=Egyptology|1=Egyptology}} hieroglyphic {{ill|Writing system|en|Writing system|qid=Q8192|lt=writing system|1=Writing system}} systems that deviate from the usual forms of representation and, due to their difficult reading, can only be deciphered by a small circle of {{ill|Egyptology|lt=Egyptologists|de|Ägyptologe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptologists|qid=Q145903|1=Egyptology}}. The assumption that reading should also be made difficult for the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egyptians|1=Ancient Egypt}} is only occasionally attested in religious texts but can be ruled out for other cases. The main distinguishing feature of different epochs is the rare use in the third and second millennium BC, while in the Late Egyptian {{ill|Orthography|en|Orthography|qid=Q43091|lt=orthography|1=Orthography}}, the system was frequently applied and led to the further development of a unique sub-cryptography . == Classifications == === Writing Styles === Older cryptography appears in both short and simple inscriptions as well as in longer religious texts. While the simple usage is mainly decorative and {{ill|Innovation|en|Innovation|qid=Q174165|lt=innovative|1=Innovation}} in nature, religious texts exhibit fixed systems that use a significantly reduced set of symbols and—wherever identifiable—show no decorative purpose. Compared to {{ill|Cosmology|en|Cosmology|qid=Q338|lt=cosmological|1=Cosmology}} depictions, an almost identical usage is observed. It is therefore assumed that information was specifically encrypted in these texts. Further distinctions emerge in the systems of {{ill|Underworld|en|Underworld|qid=Q154365|lt=Underworld books|1=Underworld}} (except for {{ill|Amduat|en|Amduat|qid=Q460509|lt=Amduat|1=Amduat}}) and {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|qid=Q4489450|lt=celestial books|1=Heaven}}. {{HieroglyphenImText|All underworld books use as a graphical {{ill|Leitmotif|en|Leitmotif|qid=Q221117|lt=leitmotif|1=Leitmotif}} the {{ill|Orthoptera|en|Orthoptera|qid=Q167810|lt=locust|1=Orthoptera}} <hiero>L4</hiero>, the plant sign <hiero>M2</hiero>, and the fabric hieroglyph <hiero>S28</hiero>. Since the set of symbols is otherwise strictly defined and limited, the frequent use of these hieroglyphs is particularly striking. In the Amduat, {{ill|Book of Nut|en|Book of Nut|qid=Q2005559|lt=Nutbook|1=Book of Nut}}, and in the books of day and night, however, the locust does not appear, the plant sign appears only occasionally and irregularly (sporadically), and the fabric hieroglyph is found only in the Amduat. The system of the underworld books appears partially in independent texts, for example in the underworld book of {{ill|Tutankhamun|en|Tutankhamun|qid=Q12154|lt=Tutankhamun|1=Tutankhamun}},<ref>Alexandre Piankoff: ''The shrines of Tut-Ankh-Amon''. Pantheon Books, New York 1955, pp. 120–131.</ref> or is only sporadically attested (33rd scene in the {{ill|Book of Gates|en|Book of Gates|qid=Q32137|lt=Book of Gates|1=Book of Gates}}, judgment hall of {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|qid=Q46491|lt=Osiris|1=Osiris}}).|qq=}} === Sounds "r," "l," and "d" === Particularly in the areas of {{ill|shadow clocks|de|Schattenuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shadow clocks|qid=Q2302073|1=Ancient Egyptian sundial}} and in the Nutbook, the spelling of the ancient Egyptian "3," represented by the hieroglyphs <hiero>D36:D36</hiero>, for the phonetic value "r" is striking. This cryptogram is based on historical {{ill|Phonetics|en|Phonetics|qid=Q35395|lt=phonetics|1=Phonetics}}. On the one hand, this spelling was used in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q177819|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} to represent the sound "d," while on the other hand, the ancient Egyptian "r" was close to "d." The first attestation of <hiero>D36:D36</hiero> to represent the {{ill|Semitic languages|de|Semitische Sprache|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Semitic languages|qid=Q34049|1=Semitic languages}} "d" is in the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom of Egypt|1=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}}. Conversely, the Semitic "d" was used for the ancient Egyptian "r." This practice explains the phonetic similarity of the hieroglyphs used.<ref>Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne.'' Copenhagen 2007, p. 32.</ref> Additionally, in the {{ill|Execration texts|de|Ächtungstexte|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Execration texts|qid=Q252811|1=Execration texts}}, the ancient Egyptian "3" was used exclusively for writing the Semitic "r" and "l." With the beginning of the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom (Egypt)|1=New Kingdom of Egypt}} after the {{ill|Second Intermediate Period of Egypt|en|Second Intermediate Period of Egypt|qid=Q206715|lt=Second Intermediate Period|1=Second Intermediate Period of Egypt}}, these spelling variations disappeared in the {{ill|Late Egyptian language|de|Neuägyptische Sprache|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Egyptian language|qid=Q1852329|1=Late Egyptian language}}. == Literature == * {{LÄ|II|1196||Hieroglyphs, Section H: Cryptography|[[Henry George Fischer]]|qq=}} * {{ill|Jan Assmann|en|Jan Assmann|qid=Q61247|lt=Jan Assmann|1=Jan Assmann}}: ''On the Aesthetics of the Secret. Cryptography as Calligraphy in Ancient Egypt.'' In: {{ill|Susi Kotzinger|de|Susi Kotzinger|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susi Kotzinger|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (Ed.): ''Signs between Clear Text and Arabesque; Conference of the Konstanz Graduate College "Theory of Literature" held in October 1992.'' (= ''International Research on General and Comparative Literature.'' Vol. 7) Rodopi, Amsterdam 1994, ISBN 90-5183-728-3, pp. 175–186. ([http://archiv.ub.uni-heidelberg.de/propylaeumdok/volltexte/2015/2582 online]) * John-Coleman Darnell: ''The enigmatic netherworld books of the Solar-Osirian Unity. Cryptographic compositions in the tombs of Tutankhamun, Ramesses VI, and Ramesses IX.'' Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Göttingen 2004, ISBN 3-525-53055-2. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qid=Q2643704|1=Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so-called Nutbook.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. == References == <references /> {{SORTIERUNG:Ancient Egyptian Cryptography|qq=}}
{{translated page|de|Altägyptische Kryptographie|version=213731826|comment=Imported full de history}}
